item.title: Russia Says Dagestan Region Sees Blackouts After Severe Storms
item.description: Heavy rains, wet snow and strong winds damaged some power facilities in the Russian region of Dagestan, leaving dozens of villages without electricity, local officials said.

item.title: Sydney’s E-Bike Boom Hits the Brakes as Injuries Rise
item.description: Serious injuries are on the rise, and authorities are increasingly concerned about high-powered models.

item.title: US Seeks to Streamline Offshore Management in Regulator Overhaul
item.description: The US Interior Department said it’s merging two oversight agencies to create a Marine Minerals Administration and move toward a “more modern, coordinated approach” to managing offshore resources, including critical minerals.

item.title: What a Clean Cookstove Company’s Bankruptcy Means for Carbon Markets
item.description: Prices on Corsia, a marketplace for airlines, have fallen in the months following Koko’s collapse.

item.title: France Plans to Award 10 Offshore and Floating Wind Projects by Early 2027
item.description: France plans to hold a big tender for 10 offshore wind projects, and announce winners by late this year or early 2027, as part of its green push.

item.title: Dry Winter Saps Norway’s Hydropower Potential
item.description: The country acts as Europe’s battery, but it doesn’t have enough charge after below-normal snowfall.

item.title: Neoen to Build France’s Largest Battery Amid Strained Power Grid
item.description: Renewable energy developer Neoen will build France’s largest battery, helping to store rising solar and wind generation and ease strain on the country’s grid.

item.title: A Winter Without Snow Depletes Europe’s Clean Energy Reservoir
item.description: Hydropower, a key export from Norway, is being tested by the country’s driest winter in years, pushing electricity prices higher.

item.title: How a Company Collapse Is Denting Trust in Carbon Markets – Again
item.description: This year was supposed to be a turning point for carbon markets, with the United Nations’ long-delayed country-to-country trading system coming into force and airlines preparing to enter a mandatory program to offset their emissions.

item.title: Climate Tech is Making Europe More Resilient to Energy Shocks
item.description: The world is facing a second energy crisis in less than five years. Is it better prepared this time? Investor Aurore Belfrage joins the Zero podcast to discuss.

item.title: Fund Sees ‘Significant’ Opportunity in India’s New Climate Plan
item.description: India’s new emissions-reduction plans offer significant investment opportunities in sectors related to grid upgrades and storage batteries, according to one of the country’s largest climate-focused funds.

item.title: The US Data Center Boom Is Hitting a Transformer Crunch
item.description: More than half the US data centers planned for this year are expected to be delayed.

item.title: Gas Importer VNG Urges Germany to Intervene to Refill Storage
item.description: Gas grid operator VNG AG has urged the German government to take action to make sure storage is refilled with price spreads uneconomical amid high prices driven by the Middle East war.

item.title: Europe Braces for Wild Weather and Cold Snaps Through April
item.description: Europe faces weeks of volatile spring weather, with competing atmospheric factors raising the risk of cold spells, storms and late-season snow.

item.title: BYD Exports Jump On Iran Oil Shock as Domestic Slump Persists
item.description: BYD Co.’s exports and overseas sales climbed 65% in March as surging oil prices due to the Iran war boosted demand for electric cars, though the automaker continues to struggle to regain momentum in the China market.

item.title: America’s AI Build-Out Hinges on Chinese Electrical Parts
item.description: The struggle to manufacture transformers, switchgear and batteries domestically has forced the US to rely on imports, delaying data center construction.

item.title: FirstRand Sells Bond Linked to Cape Water Catchment Performance
item.description: FirstRand Ltd. sold a 2.5 billion rand ($149 million) bond that rewards investors based on improvements in a Cape Town water-catchment area, measured by the removal of invasive vegetation.

item.title: EU Weighs Ukraine Crisis Strategy to Calm High Energy Prices
item.description: The European Union will consider reopening a set energy measures used after Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine as the bloc seeks to contain a fresh price shock tied to the Iran war.

item.title: How a Wind Turbine Blessing Turned Into a Nightmare in Texas
item.description: A company promised to recycle old blades. It failed to deliver, leaving a small town to figure out what to do next.

item.title: India Set for Searing Summer as Iran War Strains Energy Supplies
item.description: Most of India is forecast to experience higher than normal heat wave days through June, raising the risk of power shortages as the South Asian nation grapples with energy strains worsened by the Middle East conflict.

item.title: Thousands of Dumped Wind-Turbine Blades Prompt Crackdown in Texas
item.description: Residents of Sweetwater, Texas, are frustrated that the old blades have sat in the town for years. Authorities have stepped up efforts to hold someone responsible.

item.title: Vietnam LNG Power Project Eyes Green Pivot on Soaring Gas Prices
item.description: Vingroup JSC has asked the Vietnamese government to allow it to replace a large liquefied natural gas power project with renewable energy, citing surging fuel prices linked to the Middle East conflict.

item.title: Fire Hotspots, Scant Rain Raise Haze Alarm in Southeast Asia
item.description: The number of fire hotspots across Indonesia and Malaysia is at the highest in seven years, raising the risk of severe haze conditions across the region in the coming months.

item.title: Japan’s Top Polluters Face New Rules as Carbon Market Advances
item.description: Major companies in Japan face mandatory new rules on climate reporting under the next phase in the country’s planned national emissions trading system.

item.title: China Steel Mills Could Boost Profits With Green Push, CREA Says
item.description: A shift toward greener steel production could help China’s mills to restore profitability that’s been eroded by years of overcapacity and weak domestic demand, according to a think tank.

item.title: The Group That Decides Endangered Species’ Fate
item.description: The so-called “God Squad” is set to weigh an exemption that would allow oil and gas production in the Gulf of Mexico to sidestep environmental protections.

item.title: Germany Warns EU Methane Rules Risk Hindering Gas Imports
item.description: Germany warned the European Union’s rules to curb methane emissions from oil and gas imports risk impeding crucial LNG purchases just as the country seeks to diversify suppliers and cushion the Middle East war’s impact on energy prices.

item.title: California’s Thin Snowpack Raises Summer Water Risks
item.description: The state’s snowpack is the lowest in years, after an unusually warm winter.

item.title: Why the Endangered Species ‘God Squad’ Is Meeting After 35 Years
item.description: In an executive order on his first day back in office, President Donald Trump directed an obscure panel of senior federal agency leaders called the Endangered Species Committee to start meeting regularly. The committee has the power to override certain legal protections for endangered species put at high risk by proposed industrial projects. In other words, it has the power to effectively condemn a species to extinction, earning the group a nickname: the God Squad.

item.title: EU Short-Term Carbon Amendment Won’t Change Reserve Parameters
item.description: The European Union plans to keep key parameters of its mechanism that controls supply in the carbon market unchanged when it makes a short-term revision to help contain high power prices.
